description:
In 2024, health insurance companies rejected health claims worth ₹26,000 crores in India.
Why? Because most health policies have hidden rules that no one tells you about — like sub-limits, room rent caps, co-payment, and more.

This video covers 9 commonly found clauses in health insurance policies that may limit your claim. Some of these clauses can be restrictive, while others serve important functions within the insurance system.

Please note, not all the rules discussed in this video are inherently negative. The “awful meter” is used purely as a storytelling device to help viewers understand the potential impact of each clause.

For instance, the Reasonable and Customary Charges clause exists to protect insurers from inflated or excessive billing and helps maintain affordable premiums for policyholders.

Similarly, day care treatments — which refer to procedures requiring less than 24 hours of hospitalisation due to medical advancements — are now included in most health insurance plans. However, if such coverage is missing from your policy, it may be worth reconsidering your choice.

I will add some more value to this video. Download your Insurance Document. Turn on Temporary mode in ChatGPT. Use model 4.1 (experimental) and ask write this prompt:

"Act as a meticulous insurance analyst and carefully review the provided health insurance policy document. Your task is to break down the document into clear, concise sections while highlighting critical nuances, fine print, and potential pitfalls. Ensure the analysis is structured for easy understanding, using plain language and avoiding jargon unless explained. Address the following elements in detail:  

1. *Coverage Scope*  
   - Explicitly list covered services (e.g., hospitalization, prescriptions, mental health, preventive care).  
   - Identify exclusions (e.g., experimental treatments, cosmetic procedures, pre-existing condition waiting periods).  
   - Clarify coverage limits (e.g., annual/lifetime maximums, per-service caps).  

2. *Cost Breakdown*  
   - Detail premiums, deductibles, co-pays, co-insurance rates, and out-of-pocket maximums.  
   - Highlight less obvious costs (e.g., penalties for out-of-network care, balance billing risks, non-covered services).  

3. *Network Restrictions*  
   - Explain in-network vs. out-of-network rules, including cost disparities.  
   - Note requirements for referrals, pre-authorizations, or approvals for specialists, procedures, or emergency care.  

4. *Claims Process*  
   - Outline steps to file claims, required documentation, and submission deadlines.  
   - Describe appeal procedures for denied claims and timelines for resolution.  

5. *Renewal & Termination Clauses*  
   - Specify policy renewal terms, automatic renewal conditions, and premium increase triggers.  
   - Flag circumstances allowing the insurer to cancel coverage (e.g., fraud, non-payment grace periods).  

6. *Hidden Clauses*  
   - Identify subrogation rights, coordination of benefits with other plans, or mandatory arbitration agreements.  
   - Highlight ambiguous phrasing (e.g., "medically necessary," "reasonable and customary charges").  

7. *Red Flags*  
   - List overly broad exclusions, unusually short claim windows, or excessive documentation demands.  
   - Warn about gaps in critical coverage (e.g., maternity care, mental health, chronic conditions).  

8. *Actionable Guidance*  
   - Recommend verifying provider network status, tracking out-of-pocket expenses, and contesting unclear terms.  
   - Suggest consulting a legal/insurance expert for ambiguous clauses.  

Present findings in a structured format with numbered sections, bullet points, and bolded key terms. Prioritize clarity and urgency, ensuring the user grasps risks and obligations without overwhelm."

comments.text:
Summary for [Insurance companies are HIDING this from YOU!](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nqOCyELuNCA) by [Merlin AI](https://merlin.foyer.work/)

Understanding Hidden Clauses in Health Insurance: Avoid Claim Rejection with These Key Insights for 2025.

[00:03](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nqOCyELuNCA&t=3) Beware of hidden clauses in health insurance policies that can lead to claim rejections.
- Over 26,000 crores in health insurance claims were rejected in 2024 due to hidden clauses in policies.
- Sublimits in policies can drastically reduce payouts, and room rent capping can further complicate claims.

[01:49](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nqOCyELuNCA&t=109) Understanding room rent clauses in health insurance is crucial.
- Room rent clauses specify limits on hospital accommodation, potentially affecting overall claims.
- Proportionate deduction can lead to unexpectedly high out-of-pocket expenses when exceeding room rent limits.

[03:44](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nqOCyELuNCA&t=224) Choose health insurance with no room rent cap for full benefits.
- Room rent caps can lead to shared costs between the insurer and insured, reducing coverage efficiency.
- No claim bonuses increase the sum assured but may not impact capped room rent calculations.

[05:31](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nqOCyELuNCA&t=331) Beware of restoration benefits and co-payment clauses in health insurance.
- Restoration benefits can be restricted by insurers, preventing reuse for the same disease.
- Co-payment requires you to pay a percentage of bills or a fixed deductible, potentially increasing out-of-pocket expenses.

[07:25](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nqOCyELuNCA&t=445) Understand the impact of differential pricing and waiting periods on health insurance.
- Insurance companies utilize differential pricing based on medical expense averages in various cities, affecting premiums offered.
- Be aware of initial waiting periods and pre-existing condition clauses that can limit coverage when filing claims.

[09:09](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nqOCyELuNCA&t=549) Choosing the right health insurance plan is crucial for financial safety.
- Understand the difference between regular top-up plans and super top-up plans to avoid maximum out-of-pocket expenses.
- Ensure that daycare procedures are covered in your policy for treatments requiring less than 24 hours of hospitalization.

[10:57](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nqOCyELuNCA&t=657) Health insurance policies may have restrictive clauses affecting coverage.
- Some insurance policies require hospitalization for at least 24 hours, excluding daycare procedures.
- Cashless benefits streamline claims, but out-of-network hospitals can force patients to pay upfront.

[12:55](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nqOCyELuNCA&t=775) Insurance companies set limits on reasonable and customary treatment costs.
- Insurance policies often include clauses stating they will only cover costs deemed reasonable for specific treatments.
- Customers have limited recourse against hospitals and insurance companies regarding charges that may exceed customary rates.

comments.text:
The intent is good, but the portrayal of certain restrictions is completely wrong. 

Before I start, let me tell you that we are your big fans Pratik. I have been personally following your journey since some time, and I and we have learned so much from you. 

When you read this don't get us wrong, we are ourselves massive critiques of the insurance space - to the extent that we are 100% independent (we don't take any commissions or ads from insurers), but we want to point this out that there is a serious lack of deep research and perspective in this video. 

Yes, you may be doing this to simplify things for your audience, but because you are coming from Zerodha you have a larger responsibility, than maybe a regular Youtuber. 


For instance: 

1. Reasonable & Customary clause - is required because hospitals don't have billing regulations in our country - and hence when a hospital decides to overcharge (which is quite common) insurers are on the receiving end. Thus, the introduction this clause. It's important to give perspective of why certain clauses are there - before you call them awful, for effect. Remember insurers need to sustain financially to be survive, profit to be able to pay your rightful claims. It is not an evil clause, unless it is misused (which can be the case with any contractual clause that way). Your point that one will have to compare rate cards - is not possible, but the industry has to work under the ground realities and challenges, and thus this clause. 

2. Not having cashless - How can an insurer have a contract with every hospital, bro. It's like why does Zomato not have a particular restaurant. It's a contract between two parties, and even one of them can decline the arrangement. The brighter side is that insurers are not saying you need to just go to our contracted hospitals - but also to other hospitals and we will pay you after you submit the bills. This is a feature not a bug. 

2. No Day Care treatment. There are no insurance policies that do not not cover Day Care. There are restrictions with regards to number of day care yes, but there are no mainstream policies that your audience will buy that don't cover DayCare. Also the definition you have mentioned about Day Care is wrong. Not all hospitalizations less than 24 hours are covered. They need to be less than 24 hours due to technological advancements. 

Yes - a few of these are nuances, and that's what a research backed video, from a responsible content creator should have. That's what I would expect as audience.  

Mahavir Chopra
Founder, @Beshak

comments.text:
Points to check when buying a health insurance:
✓Sub limit: check the amount company is paying for the treatment when you get admitted to hospital.
Fir example: company has a sub limit of 2 lacks for heart surgery and the bill is 5 Lacks. The other 3 lacks you need to pay from your pocket.
✓Room rent capping: suppose you're admitted for a treatment. The room rent capping is 5000₹ and AC room is not available only deluxe room is available which is 10000₹ a day.
Now you assume that the total bill will be paid by the insurer and only room rent will be shared 50-50%. But 'NO'. The insurer will say everything including all treatment costs will be 50-50.
✓No claim bonus: suppose you've a 20% no claim bonus. That means if didn't claim insurance last year, this year your insurance will be 20% more. But the room rent is SAME.!
Suppose you've a room rent of 1% on 10 lacks policy. It'll be the same even if get a bonus of 20%
✓Restoration benefit: suppose you've a 10 lack ₹ policy and you finish all that and this benefit will make sure you get refilled with the same amount but it's not. Some insurers don't allow it to be used for the same disease.!
✓Co-payment: Avoid co-payment clause.!
Avoid Zonal co-payment.!
✓ Waiting period: Make sure this's as low as possible.! Every insurance policy has this.
✓Top up: Suppose you've a 10 lacks policy and you buy a 5 lacks top up. 
Suppose you get admitted to the hospital and the bill is 10 lacks. The insurer will pay this amount.
Suppose you get admitted again and the bill is 5 lacks. Thr top up only gets activated once the bill crosses 10 lacks. So the insurer will not pay anything.!
Instead you do a super top up.!
✓Day care procedure: The insurer may say you need to admit at least for 24 hours to get a claim.
✓Cashless and reimbursement: The insure might say the hospital is not in our network area and ask you to pay and ask insurer later.!
✓Reasonable treatment costs: In India we don't have standard rates for treatment. The hospital charges are different across different hospitals but the insurer might say for a surgery the standard rate is 2-3 lacks and we'll only pay that much.!
In case of a pre planned hospitalisation. You first check the treatment costs and compare. If you still prefer some hospital ove the other, try to negotiate or be ready to pay the remaining charges.!

comments.text: The whole concept of medical insurance is a big fraud in India. Marketing and PR agencies will market their products as your life is nothing without medical insurance. Medical insurance is a good thing if implemented properly but unfortunately in India, they are scammers and cheaters. You will hardly get the treatment covered in that. There are hundreds of conditions on the treatments. Sometimes, the waiting periods are 4 years and more. Instead of paying 30–40k every year for the this insurance, its better to have a dedicated fund for the medical and keep putting money there every year. Believe me in the long run, only this money will come handy. There was a case when my wife got severe pain in her abdomen and we admitted her to the hospital where she was on medication for 3 days. The insurance company asked us to get hundreds of documents, signatures, etc. to process claim and in the end they rejected it saying that your wife is 5 month pregnant so this is not covered. I had stopped using the insurances. Same is the case with term insurance. Don’t go for it.
